preamble: Debuccalization of the voiceless dental fricative [θ] to the glottal fricative [h] is a common step in the weakening of dental sounds across several language families, most notably in Ancient Greek and Finnish.

examples.note: Lenited /t/ became [θ] in Primitive Irish and subsequently debuccalized to [h] by the Middle Irish period. Example: 'athair' (father) [ˈaθirʲ] → [ˈahirʲ].
Proto-Indo-European *s shifted to *θ and then debuccalized to [h] in word-initial position.
Historical interdental fricatives debuccalized to [h] in many Western and Eastern dialects.

sources: Thurneysen, R. (1946). A Grammar of Old Irish.
